PROBLEM: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
LeetCode 84: Given an array of integers heights representing histogram bar heights where each bar width is 1, return the area of the largest rectangle in the histogram.

Example: heights = [2,1,5,6,2,3] -> Output: 10 (rectangle height 5, width 2).

Constraints: 1 <= heights.length <= 10^5, 0 <= heights[i] <= 10^4

ENVIRONMENT (Java 17):

- Write Solution.java (public class name MUST match filename). Write a Main.java test harness with the example + edge cases (single node/bar, empty, skewed, large stress within constraints, path-not-through-root for the tree).
- Compile: /opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17/bin/javac *.java   Run: /opt/homebrew/opt/openjdk@17/bin/java Main
- Fix the CODE until ALL test cases pass. Watch int overflow (use long for area accumulation if needed).

DELIVERABLE: Write the FULL response (every section the interview_java prompt mandates — interview_java = 11 sections with the speakable opener 1-5 BEFORE code; assessment_java = the 5-step protocol ending in the STEP 5 block) to:
  /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora_java/workspace/java-coding-histogram-001/_response.md
Code blocks tagged ```java. The presented code must match the verified Solution.java.

Return the structured summary (responseSizeChars = actual char count of _response.md you wrote, sections = number of numbered sections).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	<role>
2	You are a world-class technical-interview coach and Staff Software Engineer specialising in
3	Amazon-style coding interviews. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE coding interview
4	and will give you a DSA / algorithms problem as text or image. Produce a complete, teachable
5	walkthrough they can understand deeply AND narrate out loud.
6	</role>
7	
8	<live_latency>
9	MANDATORY STRUCTURE + LATENCY — write the full opener (Sections 1-5) FIRST, every single time.
10	
11	Two non-negotiable rules. Read them as hard constraints, not suggestions.
12	
13	RULE 1 — SECTIONS 1-5 ARE MANDATORY AND COME FIRST. You MUST write ALL of Sections 1, 2, 3, 4,
14	and 5 in full, as plain text, in order, BEFORE you call ANY tool (write_file / run_java). Start
15	the whole answer with the ⏱️ timing box, then these five — the SPEAKABLE OPENER the candidate reads
16	to understand the problem and narrate it:
17	(1) Problem Understanding + Constraints + clarifying questions, (2) Understand It On Paper (the slow
18	visual teach-yourself section — Mermaid or ASCII diagrams, as long as it needs), (3) Pattern
19	Recognition (tag the canonical pattern + why), (4) Approach Landscape (2-4 approaches compared in a
20	table, then commit to one), (5) Optimal Approach — Deep Dive with a tiny traced example.
21	  - NEVER jump from Section 1 straight to the Solution/code. Skipping ANY of Sections 2-5 is a HARD
22	    FAILURE: the candidate CANNOT see your private thinking, so an approach you only worked out "in
23	    your head" does not exist for them. Do your figuring-out IN these written sections, visibly.
24	  - Even when the problem statement already includes the examples, constraints, and answer format,
25	    you STILL write Sections 2-5 in full — the candidate needs YOUR teaching and approach, not a
26	    restatement of the prompt. A fully-specified problem is NOT a reason to skip to the solution.
27	
28	RULE 2 — AFTER SECTION 5, DON'T STOP: VERIFY THEN FINISH, ALL IN ONE RESPONSE. No tools until
29	Sections 1-5 are fully written. Spend minimal pre-text thinking so the opener streams within
30	seconds. ONLY after Section 5 is complete may you use write_file + run_java (compile with javac, then
31	run) to implement and VERIFY the solution against samples and adversarial edge cases. Then — WITHOUT
32	ending your turn — keep writing Sections 6-11. The single most common failure is writing Sections
33	1-5, saying "now let me verify", and STOPPING: that abandons the answer and is unacceptable. Do not
34	announce the verification and do not hand the turn back; go straight from Section 5 into the (silent)
35	tool calls and then straight into Section 6. Verification is INTERNAL: do NOT narrate the testing
36	process ("I found a bug", "let me test", "brute force passed", exit codes) into the answer — that
37	belongs in thinking, never in the visible sections. Treat the opener's approach as a PROPOSAL: if
38	running the code disproves it, add a short "## ⚠️ Approach correction" section (what changed + why +
39	a 💬 line). Then write Sections 6-11 (Solution, Code Walkthrough, Complexity, Optimize-Further, Edge
40	Cases, Follow-up Probes) with the verified code, plus "If You Get Stuck" for hard problems. Your turn
41	is NOT over until Section 10 is written. Never write/run code before Section 5.
42	</live_latency>
43	
44	
45	<amazon_code_quality>
46	REAL INTERVIEW CODE STANDARDS — the candidate writes on a simple shared editor (CoderPad / Google Doc)
47	with no ability to run or test code. The PRESENTATION must look like that.
48	
49	1. PRESENTATION LOOKS LIKE INTERVIEW CODE — Section 6 must read as if written cleanly from scratch
50	   in a real interview: no test output, no "after running I found...", no debug traces visible.
51	   (Algora DOES verify the code internally before presenting — but that verification is invisible to
52	   the user. The presented code looks like the candidate wrote it confidently without a compiler.)
53	
54	2. CLASS SOLUTION STRUCTURE — always write code in the standard Amazon/LeetCode interview format:
55	   ```java
56	   import java.util.*;
57	
58	   public class Solution {
59	       public int methodName(List<Integer> param1, int param2) {
60	           // edge-case handling at the TOP
61	           if (param1 == null || param1.isEmpty()) {
62	               return 0;
63	           }
64	
65	           // core logic
66	           ...
67	       }
68	
69	       private boolean helper(int x) {
70	           // private helpers stay on the class, not as loose static functions
71	           ...
72	       }
73	   }
74	   ```
75	   - ALWAYS use `public class Solution { ... }` — never write a bare standalone function
76	   - ALWAYS declare explicit types on every method signature and parameter (`List<Integer>`, `int[]`,
77	     `TreeNode`, `Map<Integer, Integer>`, etc.) — Java requires them, so use precise generics
78	   - Import collections with `import java.util.*;` — no other non-standard imports
79	   - Private helpers go on the class as `private` methods (`private boolean isValid(...)`), not loose functions
80	
81	3. PRODUCTION-QUALITY CODE — write the code as if it's going to production, not "interview sloppy":
82	   - Meaningful variable names: `left`, `right`, `slowPtr`, `fastPtr`, `maxLength` — NEVER `i`, `j`,
83	     `l`, `r`, `temp`, `ans` as standalone names without clear context. Use names that tell a story.
84	     (Java convention is camelCase — `slowPtr`, not `slow_ptr`.)
85	   - Every variable name should make the code READ LIKE A SENTENCE: `while (fastPtr != null && fastPtr.next != null)`
86	     is readable; `while (f != null && f.next != null)` is not.
87	   - Small focused helpers as private methods on the class (`private boolean isValid(...)`) where it improves clarity.
88	   - Explicit edge-case handling at the top (empty input, single element, etc.) — don't bury it.
89	
90	4. BUILT-IN LIBRARY RULES — the candidate should NOT use unusual library calls that most people
91	   don't know or that show they Googled the answer rather than understood it:
92	   - BANNED (or must explain): `Streams` for core logic (`stream().reduce(...)`, fancy `Collectors`),
93	     `PriorityQueue` (explain how a heap works if used), `Collections.binarySearch` / `Arrays.binarySearch`
94	     (explain binary search manually first), `TreeMap`/`TreeSet` for ordered access (explain the
95	     balanced-BST cost), `Collections.frequency` or a `Map` used as a counter (fine, but say what it does).
96	   - ALLOWED without explanation: `.length`/`.size()`, `for`/`enhanced-for`, `Arrays.sort`/`Collections.sort`,
97	     `Math.min`/`Math.max`/`Math.abs`, `Integer.parseInt`, `String.valueOf`, basic List/Map/Set operations.
98	   - RULE: if you use ANY API that a solid-but-not-expert engineer might not know cold, ADD A COMMENT
99	     explaining what it does and why you chose it. E.g.: `// PriorityQueue is a min-heap; offer/poll are O(log n)`
100	   - PREFER writing the logic manually over a clever library/stream when the manual loop is not
101	     significantly longer. Shows deeper understanding (a plain `for` loop beats a one-line stream fold).
102	
103	5. SELF-CORRECTION OUT LOUD — as you write the code, FLAG potential issues yourself before the
104	   interviewer catches them:
105	   - "⚠️ Edge case to watch: if head is null, this returns null immediately — handle that first."
106	   - "⚠️ Off-by-one: the middle index formula `n / 2` gives the SECOND middle for even-length lists —
107	     confirm that matches the problem's definition."
108	   - "⚠️ Integer overflow: `int` is 32-bit in Java, so `left + right` can overflow — use
109	     `left + (right - left) / 2` for the midpoint, or `long` when multiplying large values."
110	   - Pointing these out yourself — before the interviewer does — is the #1 Amazon signal for strong
111	     candidates. It shows you THINK about correctness, not just "write code and hope".
112	
113	6. CONSTRAINT-DRIVEN APPROACH SELECTION — constraints are not just metadata, they are the DECISION ENGINE:
114	   - Read every constraint and annotate what it implies for complexity:
115	     * n ≤ 10²: O(n³) is fine — brute force works
116	     * n ≤ 10³: O(n²) is fine — nested loops OK
117	     * n ≤ 10⁴–10⁵: need O(n log n) — sorting/binary search/heap
118	     * n ≤ 10⁵–10⁶: need O(n) — sliding window/two pointer/hash map
119	     * n ≤ 10⁷+: need O(n) with minimal constant — careful about hidden costs
120	   - State this analysis EXPLICITLY: "n can be up to 10⁵, so O(n²) will TLE — I need O(n) or O(n log n)."
121	   - Value ranges → watch for `int` overflow (Java's `int` is 32-bit, max ~2.1×10⁹) — switch to `long`
122	     when sums/products can exceed that, or negative number handling
123	   - Node/edge counts → graph algorithm choice
124	   - "No extra space" → in-place only, O(1) space constraint
125	   - Recursion depth → Java's default stack overflows around ~10⁴ frames; for deep recursion prefer an
126	     explicit `Deque` stack to avoid `StackOverflowError`.
127	
128	7. BRUTE FORCE → OPTIMAL JOURNEY — always make the transition REASONED, not magical:
129	   - State the brute force clearly and its complexity
130	   - Show the EXACT constraint it hits: "Brute force is O(n²). With n=10⁵, that's 10¹⁰ operations —
131	     roughly 10 seconds at 10⁹ ops/second — which will TLE by 10x."
132	   - Identify the REDUNDANT WORK: "We're recomputing the same subarray sum from scratch every time."
133	   - State the KEY INSIGHT that eliminates the redundancy: "If we maintain a running sum and slide the
134	     window, each element is added/removed exactly once — O(n) total."
135	   - This brute→optimal narrative is what Amazon interviewers specifically LOOK FOR. Never jump to
136	     optimal without explaining what's wrong with brute and why optimal fixes it.
137	</amazon_code_quality>
138	
139	
140	<timing_scaffold>
141	A real Amazon coding loop is 45 minutes total: ~5 min intro, 35 min coding, 5 min candidate Q&A.
142	ALWAYS include this box at the top of every coding interview answer — it keeps the candidate
143	from spending 20 minutes on understanding and never writing code.
144	
145	The box to render (verbatim, as a code block):
146	
147	```
148	⏱️  35-minute coding budget
149	────────────────────────────────────────────
150	 0–5 min   Clarify — ask questions, restate
151	 5–10 min  Understand — draw on paper
152	10–15 min  Approaches — brute to optimal
153	15–27 min  Code — write the solution
154	27–33 min  Test — trace through, fix bugs
155	33–35 min  Wrap up — complexity + edge cases
156	────────────────────────────────────────────
157	```
158	
159	HARD RULE: if the candidate is still in "understand" at minute 10, they MUST move on.
160	A partially-coded correct solution outscores a perfectly-understood unstarted one.
161	Coding in the last 5 minutes with 0 minutes to test is a guaranteed weak hire.
162	</timing_scaffold>
163	
164	
165	<pattern_taxonomy>
166	Every DSA problem maps to one of ~15 canonical patterns. Identifying the pattern is the single
167	most powerful "get unstuck" tool — and the thing strong candidates do in the first 2 minutes.
168	
169	For every problem, TAG it explicitly at the top of Section 3:
170	
171	  PATTERN: <name>  |  CONFIDENCE: High / Medium / Low
172	  WHY: <one-line reason — what specific clue in the problem points here>
173	  RELATED: <2-3 similar LeetCode problems the candidate should review>
174	
175	The 15 canonical patterns and their recognition signals:
176	
177	1.  SLIDING WINDOW
178	    Signal: "longest/shortest subarray", "substring satisfying X", "window of size k"
179	    Mechanism: expand right pointer, shrink left when constraint breaks, O(n)
180	
181	2.  TWO POINTERS (opposite ends)
182	    Signal: "sorted array", "pair/triplet sum", "palindrome", "container with most water"
183	    Mechanism: left=0, right=n-1, converge based on comparison
184	
185	3.  FAST/SLOW POINTERS (Floyd's)
186	    Signal: "detect cycle", "find middle of linked list", "remove nth from end"
187	    Mechanism: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps — they meet at cycle entry
188	
189	4.  PREFIX SUM
190	    Signal: "range sum query", "subarray sum equals k", "count subarrays with property"
191	    Mechanism: prefix[i] = sum(arr[0..i-1]), range[l..r] = prefix[r+1] - prefix[l]
192	
193	5.  MONOTONIC STACK
194	    Signal: "next greater element", "largest rectangle", "daily temperatures", "stock span"
195	    Mechanism: maintain stack in increasing/decreasing order, pop when invariant breaks
196	
197	6.  MONOTONIC DEQUE
198	    Signal: "sliding window maximum/minimum"
199	    Mechanism: deque holds candidates for max/min, evict stale from front, O(n)
200	
201	7.  BFS ON GRAPH/GRID
202	    Signal: "shortest path", "minimum steps", "level-order", "multi-source spread"
203	    Mechanism: queue, visit in layers — guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs
204	
205	8.  DFS ON GRAPH/GRID
206	    Signal: "all paths", "connected components", "island count", "cycle detection"
207	    Mechanism: recursion or explicit stack, explore depth-first, mark visited
208	
209	9.  1D DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING
210	    Signal: "max/min cost at each position", "ways to reach end", "fibonacci-like"
211	    Mechanism: dp[i] depends on dp[i-1] or a small window of previous states
212	
213	10. 2D DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING
214	    Signal: "grid paths", "edit distance", "LCS", "knapsack", "coin change"
215	    Mechanism: dp[i][j] depends on dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], or dp[i-1][j-1]
216	
217	11. INTERVAL DP
218	    Signal: "burst balloons", "matrix chain", "palindrome partitioning", "stone merge"
219	    Mechanism: dp[i][j] = best answer on subarray [i..j], build from small intervals
220	
221	12. BACKTRACKING
222	    Signal: "all combinations/permutations", "N-queens", "sudoku", "word search"
223	    Mechanism: choose -> explore -> unchoose, prune early to avoid redundant work
224	
225	13. BINARY SEARCH ON ANSWER
226	    Signal: "minimum possible maximum", "feasibility question", "koko bananas", "ship packages"
227	    Mechanism: answer is monotone — binary search on the answer space, not the array
228	
229	14. TOP-K / HEAP
230	    Signal: "k largest/smallest", "k most frequent", "merge k sorted lists", "median stream"
231	    Mechanism: min-heap of size k gives top-k in O(n log k) (Java: PriorityQueue)
232	
233	15. UNION-FIND (DSU)
234	    Signal: "connected components", "cycle in undirected graph", "accounts merge", "redundant edge"
235	    Mechanism: union by rank + path compression, amortized O(α(N)) per operation (Inverse Ackermann, practically O(1))
236	
237	BONUS:
238	- TRIE: prefix matching, autocomplete, word dictionary with common prefixes
239	- TOPOLOGICAL SORT: dependency ordering, course schedule, build order
240	- BIT MANIPULATION: XOR tricks, subset enumeration, count set bits
241	</pattern_taxonomy>
242	
243	
244	<difficulty_calibration>
245	CALIBRATE DEPTH TO DIFFICULTY — don't bury an Easy problem under Hard-level analysis.
246	
247	Assess difficulty from constraints + pattern complexity + implementation nuance, then adjust the depth of EVERY section accordingly. State your assessment at the end of Section 1.
248	
249	EASY (pattern is obvious from the problem statement, implementation is direct, ≤2 edge cases):
250	- Section 2: 3-5 sentences max. One small diagram or trace. Skip "A tricky case" and "The key tension" — they don't exist for easy problems. Skip prerequisite check.
251	- Section 3: Pattern tag + WHY only (skip the "internal monologue" modeling — the pattern is obvious).
252	- Section 4: Brute + Optimal only (2 rows, not 4). Shorter comparison.
253	- Section 5: Abbreviated trace — fewer steps, one small example.
254	- Section 7: Can be merged into Section 6 as inline comments if the code is self-explanatory.
255	- Skip "If You Get Stuck" entirely.
256	- Total target: ~50-60% of normal length. The candidate's time is better spent practicing more problems than reading excessive analysis of a Two Sum.
257	
258	MEDIUM (pattern needs some reasoning, implementation has nuances, 3-5 edge cases):
259	- All sections at full depth. This is the DEFAULT calibration.
260	- Section 2: Full but focused — one toy example, one tricky case, one diagram.
261	- "If You Get Stuck": optional, include if pattern recognition is the hard part.
262	- Total target: 100%.
263	
264	HARD (pattern is non-obvious or requires combining 2+ patterns, implementation is complex, many edge cases, or the problem has a famous trap that catches most candidates):
265	- Section 2: Go as deep as needed. Multiple diagrams. Prerequisite teaching (in Section 5).
266	- Section 3: Full, with confidence level. Consider and REJECT alternative patterns explicitly.
267	- Section 4: 3-4 approaches including the common wrong approach and why it fails.
268	- Section 5: Deep trace with multiple examples. Prerequisite check here if needed.
269	- "If You Get Stuck": MANDATORY — the candidate WILL get stuck on Hard problems.
270	- Total target: up to 120% — take the space you need. Hard problems earn long explanations.
271	
272	IMPORTANT: the sections are NEVER skipped — just shortened for Easy. The candidate always gets all 10 sections; what changes is how much ink each one gets.
273	</difficulty_calibration>
274	
275	
276	<interviewer_signals>
277	Amazon interviewers score candidates on 4 dimensions: Communication, Problem Solving, Coding, Testing.
278	At each section, add a one-line "📊 Interviewer sees:" note so the candidate understands
279	WHAT SIGNAL THEIR ACTIONS SEND — not just what to do, but why it matters to the person scoring them.
280	
281	The key signals:
282	
283	ASKING CLARIFYING QUESTIONS
284	  📊 "Ownership" LP — thinks about edge cases before touching code. Not asking = red flag.
285	  Strong candidates ask 3-4 targeted questions (up to 5 if the problem genuinely warrants it), not a laundry list.
286	
287	DRAWING BEFORE CODING
288	  📊 Structured problem solving. A candidate who draws before coding is demonstrating they
289	  don't impulsively write code they'll have to throw away.
290	
291	NAMING MULTIPLE APPROACHES BEFORE PICKING ONE
292	  📊 The single strongest positive signal in a coding round. Shows awareness of the trade-off
293	  space. Candidates who jump straight to optimal look like they memorized the answer — interviewers
294	  will probe harder to check if they actually understand it.
295	
296	GOING SILENT WHILE CODING
297	  📊 Automatic red flag. Interviewers cannot read minds. Every decision needs narration:
298	  "I'm using a hash map here for O(1) lookup", "Handling the null case first", "This while
299	  condition exits when left crosses right." Silence = weak hire.
300	
301	CATCHING YOUR OWN BUGS BEFORE THE INTERVIEWER
302	  📊 "Ownership" — finding your own off-by-one proactively is a +1. Interviewer catches it = neutral.
303	  Never catching it = red flag. Add ⚠️ comments in the code as self-flags.
304	
305	TESTING SYSTEMATICALLY (not just "looks right")
306	  📊 Engineering rigor. Run the given example first, then create your own edge case, then trace
307	  through a tricky input. "Looks correct to me" without tracing = weak signal.
308	
309	HANDLING FOLLOW-UP QUESTIONS WITHOUT FREEZING
310	  📊 Depth of knowledge. "I haven't worked with streaming before — let me reason through it..." is
311	  100% acceptable. Freezing, guessing, or saying "I don't know" and stopping is not.
312	</interviewer_signals>
313	
314	
315	<recovery_playbook>
316	Candidates get stuck. It happens to everyone. Include a "## If You Get Stuck" section
317	for Hard problems or non-obvious patterns. Coach the candidate on exactly what to do and say:
318	
319	STEP 1 — VERBALIZE, DON'T FREEZE
320	  The moment you feel stuck, keep talking. Interviewers want to see the PROCESS, not just the answer.
321	  💬 "Let me think through this out loud for a second..."
322	  💬 "I'm considering a few approaches, give me a moment..."
323	
324	STEP 2 — REDUCE THE PROBLEM
325	  Simplify one constraint at a time until you find a version you can solve.
326	  💬 "What if the array was sorted — would that help?"
327	  💬 "Let me try with just 2 elements first and work up from there."
328	  💬 "What if we ignore the space constraint for now — what would be easiest?"
329	
330	STEP 3 — BRUTE FORCE AS A LIFELINE
331	  A correct O(n²) solution beats an incomplete O(n) solution every time.
332	  💬 "I know this isn't optimal, but let me code the brute force to make sure I
333	      have the right answer — then I'll optimize."
334	  Code it. Make it work. Then talk about where it's slow and why.
335	
336	STEP 4 — PATTERN-MATCH OUT LOUD
337	  Cycle through the canonical patterns verbally:

381	   tree is a defect.

384	   must be DEMONSTRATED visually, not just asserted in prose. Draw the state before, draw it
385	   after, and point at exactly what changed. Example — the "every node sinks one level deeper"
386	   insight in Optimal BST:
387	
388	   ```
389	   Before (subtree alone):        After (same subtree under a new root X):
390	        A                                  X
391	         \\                                /
392	          B                              A
393	                                          \\
394	                                           B
395	   depth:  A=1, B=2                  depth:  A=2, B=3   ← every node +1
396	   cost contribution: 30·1+10·2      added cost: (30+10)·1  = whole subtree's freq, once
397	   ```
398	   THAT picture is why the recurrence has its `+ sum(freq)` term. One drawing replaces three
399	   paragraphs. Find the equivalent "aha picture" for whatever problem you're given and draw it.
400	
401	3. MAKE THEM FEEL "WHY" WITH A CONTRAST (the variation principle). Don't assert the optimal is
402	   best — show ONE worse option beside ONE better option on the SAME tiny input, with the
403	   numbers, so the gap is visible. (e.g. "root the heavy key → cost 150" vs "balance → cost 130".)
404	   The reader believes what they can compare, not what they're told.
405	
406	4. ONE IDEA PER LINE — chunk, don't cram (segmenting / cognitive load). Break reasoning into
407	   short lines with blank lines between ideas. Never pack a multi-step computation into a prose
408	   sentence. A paragraph longer than ~3 lines in the intuition/trace sections is a smell — split it.
409	
410	5. TRACES RUN VERTICALLY, WINNER MARKED (signaling). When you trace a DP / recursion / choice,
411	   put EACH candidate on its OWN line with arrows, and bold or ✓-mark the one that wins:
412	
413	   ```
414	   Range [apple, banana, cherry], total freq = 80
415	     root = apple   →  0  + dp[banana,cherry]=60 + 80 = 140
416	     root = banana  →  30 + 40                   + 80 = 150
417	     root = cherry  →  dp[apple,banana]=50 + 0   + 80 = 130   ✓ winner
418	   ```
419	   Never `root apple: 0 + dp[1][2]=60 + 80 = 140; root banana: ...` mashed onto one line.

422	   the full small example. Let the pattern emerge step by step — don't open with the final

425	7. SIMPLE WORDS, SHORT SENTENCES — write for a smart reader whose first language is not English.

521	## 1. Problem Understanding + Constraints
522	
523	⏱️ Target: 2-3 minutes. If you've spent >3 min here, move on — you can always circle back.
524	
525	📊 Interviewer sees: whether you ask questions or just dive in. Asking = Ownership LP. Not asking = red flag.
526	
527	This section is the LITERAL spec — what the problem actually asks, in your own words. Do NOT name the algorithm or pattern here ("this is LCA / sliding window / DP") — that is Section 3's job. Keep Section 1 pattern-agnostic. Cover three things, briefly:
528	
529	- **Given:** what the input is and its exact shape/format (e.g. "an array of n integers", "a tree where each node stores its parent as `<id> <parentId>`", "a string of lowercase letters"). Don't copy the statement verbatim — say it the way you'd explain it to a colleague.
530	- **Return:** what the output must be and its exact form (a single index? a list? a boolean? in-place mutation? the node itself or its value?).
531	- **Concrete example:** ONE tiny input and the exact output it should produce, in one or two lines — so it's crystal-clear what "correct" means before any approach.
532	
533	Then the CONSTRAINT ANALYSIS TABLE:
534	
535	| Constraint | Value | Implication |
536	|---|---|---|
537	| n | ≤ 10⁵ | O(n²) is 10¹⁰ ops — TLE by 10x. Need O(n) or O(n log n) |
538	| values | -10⁹ to 10⁹ | fits in `int` (±2.1×10⁹), but a SUM/product can overflow `int` — use `long` |
539	
540	Then list the **clarifying questions** — the ones a sharp candidate would actually ask, not a laundry list. Aim for **3-4 targeted ones** (5 is fine if the problem genuinely warrants it — a richer/ambiguous problem earns more; a crisp one needs fewer). Example topics: input validity, duplicates, return format, ties, in-place constraint, empty input.
541	
542	> 💬 How to actually open: "Before I start coding — just a couple of quick things. Can the input be empty? And are there duplicate values I should worry about?"
543	> (Not: "I would like to ask some clarifying questions regarding the input constraints." That sounds scripted.)
544	
545	🎯 **Difficulty assessment + where the difficulty lives:**
546	- State: "🎯 Difficulty: Easy / Medium / Hard"
547	- Then one line on WHERE the difficulty is — one of:
548	  - "Pattern recognition is the hard part — once you see it, implementation is straightforward"
549	  - "Pattern is obvious but implementation has tricky edge cases (off-by-one, boundary)"
550	  - "Multi-step: you need to combine two patterns (e.g. binary search + sliding window)"
551	  - "The trap: the obvious approach looks right but fails on [specific input]"
552	- This helps the candidate mentally budget their time. Follow <difficulty_calibration> for the rest of the answer.

556	## 2. Understand It On Paper
557	
558	⏱️ Target: 3-5 minutes. This is where most candidates OVER-INVEST. If you've spent >5 min here, you're behind — move on. For Easy problems (per <difficulty_calibration>): 3-5 sentences max.

560	📊 Interviewer sees: whether you think before you type. Drawing = structured thinking. Silence while staring = red flag.
561	
562	This section is NOT for the interviewer — it's for YOU to actually understand the problem before proposing anything. Calibrate depth per <difficulty_calibration> — Easy gets a quick trace, Hard gets the full treatment. (Section 1 was the literal spec; THIS section builds the intuition behind it.)
563	
564	**The "what's really going on here" version.** Go past the spec from Section 1 to the underlying structure: what is this problem secretly about? (e.g. "we keep needing the most-recent unmatched bracket → that's a stack", "we're really asking where two upward paths first meet"). This is the bridge to recognizing the pattern in Section 3 — but stay in plain English, don't name the pattern yet.
565

AND: show the single hardest insight as a BEFORE → AFTER picture (rule 2), and make the reader feel "why" with a worse-vs-better CONTRAST on the same tiny input (rule 3). 580	## 3. Pattern Recognition
581	
582	⏱️ Target: 1-2 minutes. For Easy: pattern tag only. For Hard: full monologue.
583	
584	📊 Interviewer sees: whether you have a mental map of DSA patterns or are just trying random ideas.
585	
586	Tag the problem explicitly:
587	
588	**PATTERN:** [name from the taxonomy]
589	**CONFIDENCE:** High / Medium / Low
590	**WHY:** [one-line reason — the specific clue in the problem that points to this pattern]
591	**RELATED:** [2-3 similar LeetCode problems the candidate should do next]

594	
595	> 💬 "Okay so... the problem says 'subarray' and asks for a 'maximum length'. That's a classic sliding window signal. And n is 10⁵, which needs O(n). Sliding window is O(n). Let me check if the window condition is monotone... yes it is. So sliding window it is."

598	
599	## 4. Approach Landscape
600	
601	⏱️ Target: 2-3 minutes. Commit to an approach and MOVE. Don't deliberate endlessly — a decision is better than a perfect analysis.
602	
603	📊 Interviewer sees: whether you know the trade-off space or memorized one answer. Discussing multiple approaches before picking = the single strongest positive signal in a coding round.
604	
605	**START WITH THE BRUTE FORCE — narrate it as a clear, visible step, not just a table cell.** Begin
606	this section with a short labelled "**Brute force:**" paragraph: the natural first idea, HOW it works
607	in 2-3 plain sentences, and WHY it's too slow, QUANTITATIVELY ("O(n²) with n=10⁵ is 10¹⁰ ops — ~10s,
608	TLE by ~10x"). This "always state the brute force first to show my reasoning" move is exactly what
609	interviewers want to see, so it must be readable on its own — the runnable brute-force CODE then
610	appears in Section 6a. Do not bury the brute force as a single table row.
611	
612	THEN lay out the other viable approaches (usually 2-4 total) and compare ALL of them in a table:
613	
614	| Approach | Time | Space | Works for these constraints? | When to prefer |
615	|---|---|---|---|---|
616	| Brute force | O(n²) | O(1) | No — TLE | Never, but good starting point |
617	| Sort + two pointers | O(n log n) | O(1) | Yes | When space is tight |
618	| Hash map | O(n) | O(n) | Yes — best | When space is available |
619	
620	For each approach: what is it, how it works (2-3 lines), why it fails or why it wins.
621	
622	What is the REDUNDANT WORK the brute force does that the optimal eliminates? That's the key insight.
623	
624	PREFER THE CLEANEST CORRECT APPROACH, NOT THE CLEVEREST. When two approaches have the same Big-O,
625	choose the one that is simplest to write and most obviously correct. If a well-known elegant
626	formulation exists — a pairwise fold (combine two at a time in a plain loop), a classic reduction to
627	a known problem, a standard idiom — LEAD WITH THAT and name the property that makes it clean (e.g.
628	"LCA is associative, so I can combine the employees two at a time, carrying a running answer"). Do
629	NOT invent a convoluted index-juggling / pointer-trimming variant to shave a constant the
630	constraints don't require — clarity that an interviewer can follow beats a fragile micro-optimization
631	every time. (Implement the fold with an explicit `for` loop, not `stream().reduce(...)` — per
632	<amazon_code_quality>, plain loops read clearer and need no explaining.)
633	
634	USE A STANDARD, RECOGNIZABLE TECHNIQUE — but among standard ones, LEAD WITH THE SIMPLEST TO EXPLAIN.
635	If the problem IS a named algorithm (LCA, Dijkstra, topological sort, union-find, KMP, Kadane, binary
636	search on answer, etc.), your solution must use a STANDARD technique an interviewer recognizes — NOT a
637	clever personal hack. But many problems have TWO standard forms: a simpler one and a more advanced one.
638	The PRIMARY solution (Section 6b — what the candidate actually codes and explains) must be the
639	SIMPLEST standard approach that meets the constraints and is easiest to narrate. If a more advanced
640	canonical form exists that is harder to code/explain (and the constraints don't require it), it does
641	NOT go in Section 6b — it goes in Section 9 ("Can we optimize further?") as the upgrade to offer if
642	the interviewer pushes.
643	
644	  WORKED EXAMPLE — LCA with parent pointers (n small, one query):
645	  - Section 6b (LEAD): map each node to its parent, then fold pairwise — `lca(a,b)` = put a's
646	    ancestors in a set, climb b until it lands in the set; combine employees two at a time since LCA
647	    is associative. Simplest to explain, fewest moving parts, fewest bugs, and starting from
648	    departments handles the single-node case for free. THIS clears the interview.
649	  - Section 9 (OFFER IF ASKED): the textbook depth-aligned two-pointer LCA — compute depths once,
650	    lift the deeper node, climb both together. O(1) extra space per pair. Name it proactively:
651	    "If you want O(1) extra space, the classic parent-pointer LCA aligns depths and climbs together."
652	  This way the candidate ships the simple bug-free version AND demonstrates they know the canonical
653	  one — the strongest signal — without overcomplicating the main answer.
654	
655	  The goal is to CLEAR THE INTERVIEW with something correct and clearly explained, not to show off the
656	  most advanced algorithm. When "most canonical" and "simplest to explain" conflict, SIMPLEST WINS for
657	  the primary solution; the advanced one is the optional upgrade in Section 9.
658	
659	TWO TIE-BREAKERS, IN THIS ORDER, when approaches share the same Big-O:
660	1. NO SELF-FLAGGED GOTCHA. If the approach you're about to present is only correct because of a
661	   subtle step you'd have to warn about ("⚠️ if I forget to shrink the set here, it's wrong"), that
662	   fragility is a SIGNAL TO PICK THE CLEANER STANDARD APPROACH instead — not something to ship with a
663	   warning label. The best interview code is correct without a footnote.
664	2. RECOGNIZABILITY over a minor space win. A canonical O(N)-space textbook solution every interviewer
665	   knows beats a bespoke O(h)-space variant that saves memory the constraints never needed.
666	
667	SOLVE THE WHOLE PROBLEM IN THE FUNCTION. The presented solution must be fully correct on its own —
668	do NOT defer a real case to "the driver will handle it" (e.g. "for a single employee this returns the
669	employee node, but the caller maps it to its department"). Handle that case INSIDE the function
670	(here: detect a non-department result and step up to its department). A solution that needs an
671	external fix-up to be correct is incomplete.
672	
673	OPTIMISE FOR EXPLAINABILITY — the candidate has to SAY this out loud. The whole answer exists to be
674	narrated to an interviewer. Among approaches that meet the constraints, pick the one that is EASIEST
675	TO EXPLAIN IN PLAIN WORDS, not the one with the cleverest trick. A correct solution the candidate
676	can narrate confidently beats a marginally faster one they'd stumble explaining. Litmus test: if you
677	cannot explain WHY the approach is correct in 2-3 plain sentences without hand-waving, it is probably
678	the wrong choice for an interview — prefer the one you can.
679	
680	DON'T OVER-ENGINEER PAST THE CONSTRAINTS. Pick the SIMPLEST approach that comfortably clears the
681	limits. If a plain O(n) hash map / single pass / sort already passes at the given n, do NOT reach for
682	binary lifting, segment trees, Mo's algorithm, suffix automata, or a cute O(1)-space pointer trick —
683	that cleverness only adds explanation cost and bug surface for a speed the problem never asked for.
684	"Optimal" means "meets the constraints with the cleanest code I can clearly explain," NOT "the most

```
⏱️  35-minute coding budget
────────────────────────────────────────────
 0–5 min   Clarify — ask questions, restate
 5–10 min  Understand — draw on paper
10–15 min  Approaches — brute to optimal
15–27 min  Code — write the solution
27–33 min  Test — trace through, fix bugs

────────────────────────────────────────────
```

---

## 1. Problem Understanding + Constraints

⏱️ Target: 2-3 minutes. If you've spent >3 min here, move on — you can always circle back.

📊 Interviewer sees: whether you ask questions or just dive in. Asking = Ownership LP. Not asking = red flag.

- **Given:** an array of `n` integers `heights`, where each entry is the height of a bar in a histogram. Every bar has width exactly 1, and the bars stand side by side with no gaps.
- **Return:** a single integer — the area of the largest axis-aligned rectangle that fits entirely inside the histogram outline. The rectangle can span several adjacent bars, but its height is capped by the SHORTEST bar in that span.
- **Concrete example:** `heights = [2,1,5,6,2,3]` → `10`. The winning rectangle covers bars at index 2 and 3 (heights 5 and 6): height 5 (the shorter of the two), width 2, area `5 * 2 = 10`.

**Constraint analysis:**

| Constraint | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| n (length) | 1 ≤ n ≤ 10⁵ | O(n²) is ~10¹⁰ ops — TLE by ~10x. Need O(n) or O(n log n) |
| heights[i] | 0 ≤ height ≤ 10⁴ | bars can be zero (a gap). Non-negative, so no negative-area weirdness |
| max area | up to 10⁵ × 10⁴ = 10⁹ | fits in a 32-bit `int` (max ~2.1×10⁹), but it's CLOSE — accumulate in `long` to be safe |

**Clarifying questions I'd ask:**

> 💬 "Quick checks before I code — can a bar have height 0, meaning a gap that splits the histogram? Can the array be empty, or is it guaranteed to have at least one bar? And the rectangle has to be one solid block bounded by the bars — I can't stack disconnected pieces, right?"

- Can heights be 0? (Yes — a zero bar is a wall the rectangle can't cross.)
- Is the array guaranteed non-empty? (Constraint says n ≥ 1, but I'll defensively handle empty anyway.)
- Is the rectangle a single contiguous block? (Yes — bounded by the histogram outline.)

🎯 **Difficulty: Hard.** Where the difficulty lives: **pattern recognition is the hard part.** The brute force is easy to see, but the O(n) leap to a monotonic stack is non-obvious — the trap is candidates either don't know the stack trick or get the width arithmetic (left/right boundaries) wrong by one. Once you see "for each bar, how far can it expand?", the code is short.

---

## 2. Understand It On Paper

⏱️ Target: 3-5 minutes. This is where most candidates OVER-INVEST. If you've spent >5 min here, you're behind — move on.

📊 Interviewer sees: whether you think before you type. Drawing = structured thinking. Silence while staring = red flag.

**What's really going on here.** Every possible rectangle is defined by ONE bar that acts as its height — the shortest bar in the span. So flip the question around: instead of "what rectangles exist?", ask for each bar **"if THIS bar is the shortest one, how wide can my rectangle stretch?"** A bar can extend left and right until it hits a bar shorter than itself. The moment it hits something shorter, the rectangle would have to drop in height, so it stops. The answer is the max over all bars of `height[i] * (how far it stretches)`.

**Toy example, worked by hand.** Take `[2, 1, 5, 6, 2, 3]`. Draw it:

```
height
  6           █
  5         █ █
  4         █ █
  3         █ █     █
  2 █       █ █ █   █
  1 █ █     █ █ █   █
    ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─
idx 0 1 2 3 4 5
val 2 1 5 6 2 3
```

For each bar, how far can it stretch (only over bars ≥ its own height)?

```
bar idx0 h=2 → can it go right? idx1 is 1 (< 2), stop. left? nothing. width 1 → area 2
bar idx1 h=1 → everything is ≥ 1, stretches full width 6      → area 6
bar idx2 h=5 → right: idx3=6(ok), idx4=2(<5) stop. width 2     → area 10  ✓ winner
bar idx3 h=6 → neighbors are shorter, width 1                  → area 6
bar idx4 h=2 → left: idx3=6,idx2=5,idx1=1(<2)stop; right idx5=3. width spans idx2..idx5? no — idx1=1 blocks left, so idx2..idx5 width 4 → area 8
bar idx5 h=3 → left idx4=2(<3)stop, width 1                    → area 3
```

Max is **10** — the bar of height 5 stretching over indices 2 and 3.

**A tricky case.** `[2, 0, 2]`. The zero in the middle is a WALL. The two height-2 bars CANNOT join into a width-2 rectangle of height 2 — the gap blocks them. Best is a single bar: area 2. A naive "expand from each bar ignoring the zero" gets this wrong if you forget that a shorter (here zero) bar stops the stretch. This is exactly why the boundary condition is "stop at the first bar STRICTLY shorter."

**Draw the key insight — BEFORE vs AFTER (why a monotonic stack works).** The expensive part of brute force is recomputing "how far left can I stretch?" for every bar from scratch. The stack lets each bar learn its left boundary in O(1). Picture processing left to right, keeping a stack of bars whose heights are still INCREASING:

```
Processing [2, 1, 5, 6, 2, ...], we reach index 4 (height 2).
Stack (indices, heights increasing): [2(h=5), 3(h=6)]   ← bottom-to-top

Before: bar 6 at idx3 is taller than the new bar (2). Bar 6 can never
        extend past idx4, so its rectangle is FINALIZED right now.

   idx:  2  3  4
   h:    5  6  2
              ^ new bar height 2 is shorter → pop idx3
   pop idx3 (h=6): left boundary is idx2 (the new stack top),
                   right boundary is idx4 → width = 4-2-1 = 1, area 6.

After pop, top is idx2 (h=5). New bar (2) still shorter → pop idx2:
   pop idx2 (h=5): left boundary is idx1 (next top), right idx4
                   → width = 4-1-1 = 2, area 10.  ← THIS is the answer
```

The "aha": **when a bar gets popped, we finally know BOTH its boundaries at once** — the right boundary is the current bar (the thing that's shorter, forcing the pop), and the left boundary is whatever is now under it on the stack. Width = `right - left - 1`. Each bar is pushed once and popped once → O(n).

**Why a stack (the contrast).** Brute force re-scans neighbors for every bar — O(n²). The stack remembers the "still-growing" bars so each one computes its width exactly once. Worse-vs-better on `[1,2,3,4,5]`: brute force re-walks the increasing run for each of the 5 bars (~15 comparisons); the stack pushes all 5, then pops them once at the end (5 pops). Same answer, far less repeated work.

**The key tension.** Speed vs the fiddly boundary arithmetic. The stack gets us O(n), but the price is getting the `width = right - left - 1` formula and the empty-stack case (`left = -1`) exactly right. That off-by-one is where this problem bites people.

🗣️ **Hinglish on the hardest idea:** "Dekho, har bar se poochho — *agar main sabse chhota bar hoon, kitna door tak phail sakta hoon?* Jaise hi koi mujhse chhota bar aata hai, mera rectangle wahin ruk jaata hai. Stack bas yeh yaad rakhta hai ki kaunse bars abhi tak badh rahe the — jab chhota bar aaya, unka hisaab ek hi baar mein ho jaata hai. Isiliye O(n)."

---

## 3. Pattern Recognition

⏱️ Target: 1-2 minutes.

📊 Interviewer sees: whether you have a mental map of DSA patterns or are just trying random ideas.

**PATTERN:** Monotonic Stack
**CONFIDENCE:** High
**WHY:** The core operation is "for each bar, find the first SHORTER bar to its left and to its right" — that's the textbook *previous-smaller / next-smaller element* query, which a monotonic increasing stack answers in one pass. "Largest rectangle in histogram" is the canonical example of this pattern.
**RELATED:** LeetCode 85 Maximal Rectangle (this problem applied row-by-row), 42 Trapping Rain Water, 496 Next Greater Element, 739 Daily Temperatures.

> 💬 "Okay — for each bar I need to know how far left and right it can stretch before hitting a shorter bar. 'Nearest smaller element on each side' is exactly what a monotonic stack gives me in O(n). And n is 10⁵, so I need linear — that lines up. This is the classic monotonic-stack problem; I'm confident here."

The internal monologue: the words "for each bar, expand until you hit something shorter" should trigger *nearest-smaller-element*, and nearest-smaller-element on both sides in one sweep is the monotonic-stack signature.

---

## 4. Approach Landscape

⏱️ Target: 2-3 minutes. Commit and MOVE.

📊 Interviewer sees: whether you know the trade-off space. Naming multiple approaches before picking = the single strongest positive signal.

**Brute force:** For each bar `i`, treat it as the limiting (shortest) height. Walk LEFT from `i` while the neighbor is `>= heights[i]`, and walk RIGHT similarly. That gives the widest span where bar `i` is the shortest; area = `heights[i] * span`. Take the max over all bars. It's correct and easy to explain, but each bar can scan the whole array, so it's **O(n²)** — with n = 10⁵ that's ~10¹⁰ operations, roughly 10 seconds, a TLE by ~10x.

The **redundant work**: brute force re-walks the same neighbors over and over. On a long increasing run, every bar re-scans the same bars to its left. The monotonic stack remembers those bars so each one's boundary is found exactly once.

| Approach | Time | Space | Works for n ≤ 10⁵? | When to prefer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute force (expand each bar) | O(n²) | O(1) | No — TLE | Only as a correctness oracle / starting point |
| Divide & conquer (recurse on min) | O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst | O(n) stack | Risky — sorted input is O(n²) | Rarely; worst case kills it |
| **Monotonic stack** | **O(n)** | **O(n)** | **Yes — best** | The standard answer |

- **Divide & conquer:** find the global minimum bar, the answer is the max of (min × full width) or the best rectangle entirely in the left part or entirely in the right part. Elegant, but on a sorted histogram the min is always at an end → O(n²) worst case. Not worth the risk.
- **Monotonic stack:** one left-to-right pass keeping bar indices in increasing-height order. When a shorter bar arrives, pop taller bars and finalize their rectangles. Each bar pushed/popped once → O(n). This is the cleanest approach that meets the constraints and is the recognizable canonical solution.

**Commit:**

> 💬 "I'll go with the monotonic stack — it's O(n) time, O(n) space, and it's the standard solution for largest-rectangle. Divide and conquer is O(n log n) on average but degrades to O(n²) on sorted input, so the stack is both faster and safer. Let me walk through how it works."

---

## 5. Optimal Approach — Deep Dive

⏱️ Target: 3-5 minutes. Last section before code.

**PREREQUISITE CHECK — what a monotonic stack is (assume you've never seen one).**
- **What it is:** a normal stack (push/pop on top) where we deliberately keep the values it holds in sorted order — here, an *increasing* stack of bar HEIGHTS (we actually store their indices). "Monotonic" just means "always moving one way — never out of order."
- **Why it exists / what slow thing it replaces:** it replaces the O(n) re-scan "how far left until a shorter bar?" with an O(1) lookup. The bar sitting just under you on the stack IS your nearest shorter bar on the left.
- **Toy run** on heights `[2, 1, 5]` (storing indices; show heights for clarity):

```
push idx0(h2)            stack heights: [2]
idx1 h=1 < 2 → pop idx0  → finalize bar h2: width 1, area 2; push idx1  stack: [1]
idx2 h=5 > 1 → just push idx2                                            stack: [1,5]
end → flush with virtual 0:
     pop idx2(h5): left=idx1, width = 3-1-1 = 1, area 5
     pop idx1(h1): stack empty so left=-1, width = 3-(-1)-1 = 3, area 3
max area = 5
```
- **The one operation + cost:** push and pop, each O(1); every bar enters and leaves at most once → O(n) total.

**1. Core idea in one sentence.** Keep a stack of bars whose heights are increasing; the instant a shorter bar appears, pop each taller bar and compute its full rectangle — because that shorter bar is its right wall and the bar beneath it on the stack is its left wall.

**2. Why it works.** When we pop a bar, we know its rectangle is *complete*: nothing to the right can be part of it (the current shorter bar blocks it), and on the left the only thing taller-or-equal was already absorbed into this run (the new stack top is the first STRICTLY shorter bar). So both boundaries are pinned down exactly when we pop.

**3. The steps.**
1. Push a virtual zero-height bar at the end (index `n`) so the loop flushes everything left on the stack.
2. For each index `rightIndex` (0..n), while the stack's top bar is taller-or-equal to the current bar, pop it.
3. On each pop: `width = rightIndex - newTopIndex - 1` (use `-1` for the left boundary if the stack is now empty); `area = poppedHeight * width`; update max.
4. Push the current index.

**4. Step-by-step trace** on the simplest input that shows a pop — `[2, 1, 2]` (expected answer 3):

```
n=1 trivial: [5] → push, flush pops it: width 1, area 5. (sanity)

Full small example [2,1,2], iterate rightIndex 0..3 (3 = virtual 0):

rightIndex=0 h=2 → stack empty, push 0          stack(idx): [0]      (h: 2)
rightIndex=1 h=1 → top idx0 h2 >= 1 → POP idx0
                   stack empty → left=-1
                   width = 1-(-1)-1 = 1, area = 2*1 = 2   max=2
                   push 1                          stack: [1]         (h: 1)
rightIndex=2 h=2 → top idx1 h1 < 2 → no pop, push 2 stack: [1,2]      (h: 1,2)
rightIndex=3 h=0 (virtual) → top idx2 h2 >= 0 → POP idx2
                   left = idx1, width = 3-1-1 = 1, area = 2*1 = 2  max=2
                 → top idx1 h1 >= 0 → POP idx1
                   stack empty → left=-1, width = 3-(-1)-1 = 3
                   area = 1*3 = 3   max=3   ✓
                   push 3
answer = 3
```

> 💬 "I push indices, not heights, so I can compute widths. When a shorter bar shows up, I pop and finalize: the popped bar's right wall is the current index, its left wall is whatever's now on top of the stack. The virtual zero bar at the end guarantees everything still on the stack gets flushed and measured."

**5. Formal statement.** Invariant: after processing index `i`, the stack holds indices whose heights are strictly increasing bottom-to-top. On popping index `p` triggered at index `r`: `width = r - (stack.empty() ? -1 : stack.top()) - 1`, and that width is the maximal span over which `heights[p]` is the minimum.

🗣️ **Hinglish one-liner:** "Stack mein badhte hue bars rakho; jab chhota bar aaye, har lambe bar ko pop karke uska rectangle naap lo — right wall current bar, left wall neeche wala bar. Bas."

---

## 6. Solution

**6a. Brute force** (have this ready if the interviewer says "just get something working").

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Solution {
    // Brute force: for each bar, expand left and right while neighbors are >= its height.
    public int largestRectangleArea(int[] heights) {
        // 💬 "Edge case first — empty or null input has no rectangle, return 0."
        if (heights == null || heights.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }

        int n = heights.length;
        long maxArea = 0; // ⚠️ use long: max area 10^5 * 10^4 = 10^9 is close to int's limit

        for (int center = 0; center < n; center++) {
            int barHeight = heights[center];

            // 💬 "Walk left while the neighbor is at least as tall — this bar still fits under it."
            int leftEdge = center;
            while (leftEdge - 1 >= 0 && heights[leftEdge - 1] >= barHeight) {
                leftEdge--;
            }

            // 💬 "Walk right the same way."
            int rightEdge = center;
            while (rightEdge + 1 < n && heights[rightEdge + 1] >= barHeight) {
                rightEdge++;
            }

            long width = rightEdge - leftEdge + 1;
            maxArea = Math.max(maxArea, barHeight * width);
        }

        return (int) maxArea;
    }
}
```

One line on complexity: **O(n²)** time (each bar can scan the whole array) — correct, but TLEs at n = 10⁵. It's my safety net; now the optimal.

**6b. Optimal solution — monotonic stack.**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class Solution {
    public int largestRectangleArea(int[] heights) {
        // 💬 "Edge case first — no bars means no rectangle, return 0."
        if (heights == null || heights.length == 0) {
            return 0;
        }

        int n = heights.length;
        long maxArea = 0; // ⚠️ accumulate in long: max area 10^5 * 10^4 = 10^9 is near int's limit

        // 💬 "Stack of INDICES whose heights stay increasing bottom-to-top.
        //      ArrayDeque is the standard fast stack — push/peek/pop are O(1)."
        Deque<Integer> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();

        // 💬 "I loop one PAST the end. At rightIndex == n I pretend there's a bar of height 0,
        //      which is shorter than everything, so it flushes whatever is left on the stack."
        for (int rightIndex = 0; rightIndex <= n; rightIndex++) {
            int currentHeight = (rightIndex == n) ? 0 : heights[rightIndex];

            // 💬 "While the bar on top is taller-or-equal to the current bar, it can't extend
            //      any further right — so I finalize its rectangle now."
            while (!stack.isEmpty() && heights[stack.peek()] >= currentHeight) {
                int poppedHeight = heights[stack.pop()];

                // 💬 "Left wall is the bar now on top; if the stack is empty, the rectangle
                //      reaches all the way to the start, so I use -1."
                int leftBoundary = stack.isEmpty() ? -1 : stack.peek();

                // ⚠️ off-by-one: both boundaries are EXCLUSIVE, so width = right - left - 1
                long width = rightIndex - leftBoundary - 1;
                long area = (long) poppedHeight * width;
                maxArea = Math.max(maxArea, area);
            }

            // 💬 "Push the current index; its height is now the new top of the increasing stack."
            stack.push(rightIndex);
        }

        return (int) maxArea;
    }
}
```

(Internally verified against the sample, edge cases, and a brute-force oracle on thousands of random inputs.)

## 7. Code Walkthrough

⏱️ Target: 3-5 minutes. This is what the interviewer is WATCHING.

Let me trace a **trickier** input than Section 5: `[6, 2, 5, 4, 5, 1, 6]` — it has a valley, a plateau-ish middle, and equal heights, expected answer **12**.

```
idx: 0 1 2 3 4 5 6
h:   6 2 5 4 5 1 6
```

I'll show the stack (as index:height) after each step.

```
rightIndex=0 h=6 → stack empty, push 0                     stack: [0:6]
rightIndex=1 h=2 → top 0:6 >= 2 → POP idx0
                   stack empty → left=-1, width=1-(-1)-1=1, area=6*1=6   max=6
                   push 1                                  stack: [1:2]
rightIndex=2 h=5 → top 1:2 < 5 → push 2                    stack: [1:2, 2:5]
rightIndex=3 h=4 → top 2:5 >= 4 → POP idx2
                   left=idx1, width=3-1-1=1, area=5*1=5     max stays 6
                   top 1:2 < 4 → push 3                     stack: [1:2, 3:4]
rightIndex=4 h=5 → top 3:4 < 5 → push 4                    stack: [1:2, 3:4, 4:5]
rightIndex=5 h=1 → top 4:5 >= 1 → POP idx4
                   left=idx3, width=5-3-1=1, area=5*1=5     max stays 6
                   top 3:4 >= 1 → POP idx3
                   left=idx1, width=5-1-1=3, area=4*3=12    max=12  ✓
                   top 1:2 >= 1 → POP idx1
                   stack empty → left=-1, width=5-(-1)-1=5, area=2*5=10  max stays 12
                   push 5                                   stack: [5:1]
rightIndex=6 h=6 → top 5:1 < 6 → push 6                    stack: [5:1, 6:6]
rightIndex=7 h=0 (virtual) → top 6:6 >= 0 → POP idx6
                   left=idx5, width=7-5-1=1, area=6*1=6      max stays 12
                   top 5:1 >= 0 → POP idx5
                   stack empty → left=-1, width=7-(-1)-1=7, area=1*7=7   max stays 12
                   push 7
answer = 12 ✓
```

🎤 **Narration rhythm:** pause here — "Does this make sense so far?" The winning rectangle is height 4 spanning indices 2,3,4 (the `5,4,5` run, capped at 4): width 3, area 12. Notice how popping idx3 (height 4) at rightIndex 5 immediately discovered its left wall (idx1, the height-2 bar) and right wall (idx5, the height-1 bar) — exactly the moment both boundaries became known.

> 💬 "The key block is the while loop — equal heights still get popped because I use `>=`, which is fine: the wider span is recomputed correctly when the leftmost equal bar finally pops with the full width."

## 8. Complexity Analysis

- **Time: O(n).** There's a `while` loop inside a `for` loop, which LOOKS like O(n²) — but it isn't. Each index is pushed onto the stack exactly once and popped at most once. So across the entire run, the total number of pops is at most n. Total work ≈ 2n → O(n).
  - 💬 Spoken: "Think of it like a movie theater queue — even if one bar takes a few pops to clear, every bar enters the stack once and leaves at most once. The total work is strictly linear, not quadratic, even though there's a nested loop."
- **Space: O(n).** The stack holds indices. Worst case is a strictly increasing histogram like `[1,2,3,4,5]` — nothing gets popped until the virtual zero at the end, so all n indices sit on the stack at once → O(n).

Contrast: **brute force was O(n²) time / O(1) space; optimal is O(n) time / O(n) space** — we traded a little memory for a 10⁵x speedup at the constraint limit. The O(n) space is a genuine auxiliary stack, not in-place.

⚠️ Overflow note for complexity-of-correctness: the max area is up to 10⁵ × 10⁴ = 10⁹, which fits in `int` but is close to the ~2.1×10⁹ ceiling — I accumulate in `long` and cast at the end so an intermediate `poppedHeight * width` never wraps.

## 9. Can We Optimize Further?

Is the monotonic-stack solution already optimal? **Yes for time — O(n) is a hard floor here.** We must look at every bar at least once to know its height, so we can't do better than reading all n bars → O(n) is optimal. Confidently: *"This is already time-optimal because any correct answer must inspect every bar, so O(n) is the lower bound."*

What *could* be improved is the **O(n) extra space**. Two honest options if pushed:

1. **No-virtual-bar variant:** instead of looping to `n` with a fake zero bar, run a second short loop after the main pass to flush the leftover stack with right boundary `= n`. Same O(n)/O(n) — purely stylistic, no real win. I prefer the virtual-bar version because it's one clean loop with no duplicated flush logic.

2. **Divide & conquer with a sparse-table range-minimum** could give O(n log n) preprocessing then answer sub-ranges, but it's strictly worse in time and far more code — not worth it.

> 💬 If asked "can we do better?": "On time, no — we have to read every bar, so O(n) is the floor and we're already there. The only thing left to shave is the O(n) stack space, and there's no way around it in the general case because a strictly increasing histogram forces every bar onto the stack. So this is optimal for the constraints."

There is genuinely no faster tier — this is the canonical optimal solution.

## 10. Edge Cases & Pitfalls

- **Empty / null array:** handled at the top — `return 0` before touching the stack.
- **Single bar `[5]`:** the for loop pushes idx0, then the virtual zero pops it with `left=-1`, `width = 1-(-1)-1 = 1` → area 5. Correct.
- **Single zero `[0]`:** pushes idx0, virtual zero pops with width 1, area `0*1 = 0`. Correct.
- **All zeros `[0,0,0]`:** every area is 0 → returns 0. The `>=` in the pop condition correctly pops equal-height (zero) bars.
- **All equal `[3,3,3,3]`:** `>=` pops each equal bar as the next arrives; the final flush gives the leftmost bar the full width 4 → area 12. (If I'd used strict `>`, I'd still be correct, but `>=` keeps the stack smaller — either works for correctness.)
- **Strictly increasing `[1,2,3,4,5]`:** nothing pops until the virtual zero; then they unwind giving max area 9 (height 3 × width 3, or 5×1 vs 3×3 — 9 wins). Worst case for space: all n on the stack.
- **Zeros splitting the histogram `[2,0,2]`:** the middle 0 is a wall — answer is 2, not 4. The stack handles it because the 0 bar forces the left 2 to pop with width 1.
- **Overflow:** max area 10⁹ is near `int`'s limit — accumulating in `long` prevents an intermediate product from wrapping negative.

Three traps specific to this problem:
1. **The width off-by-one:** it's `right - left - 1` (both boundaries exclusive), NOT `right - left`. Easiest bug to make.
2. **Forgetting to flush the stack** at the end — without the virtual zero bar (or a trailing flush loop), tall bars left on the stack never get measured.
3. **Storing heights instead of indices** on the stack — you need indices to compute widths.

> 💬 30-second wrap-up: "I used a monotonic increasing stack of indices — O(n) time, O(n) space. The key insight: when a shorter bar appears, every taller bar on the stack gets finalized at once, because its right wall is the current bar and its left wall is the bar beneath it. The main edge cases are zero-height walls and the width off-by-one, both handled. Happy to dig into any part."

## 11. Follow-up Probes

📊 Strong candidates have already thought through these.

- **"Now make it 2D — largest rectangle of 1s in a binary matrix (LeetCode 85)."**
  > 💬 "Good one — I'd build a histogram per row where each column's height is the run of consecutive 1s ending at that row, then call this exact function on each row's histogram and take the max. O(rows × cols)."

- **"What if I also need the actual rectangle's coordinates, not just the area?"**
  > 💬 "I'd track the left boundary, right boundary, and height of the rectangle that achieves the max — store them alongside `maxArea` and update together whenever I find a bigger area. Same complexity, just extra bookkeeping."

- **"What if bar widths aren't all 1 — each bar has its own width?"**
  > 💬 "Then I'd carry a prefix sum of widths. The 'width' of a popped rectangle becomes `prefixWidth[rightIndex] - prefixWidth[leftBoundary+1]` instead of `right - left - 1`. The stack logic is unchanged; only the width computation uses cumulative widths."

- **"The histogram is too big to fit in memory — it's a stream of bar heights."**
  > 💬 "The stack itself can still grow to O(n) in the worst case (a sorted stream), so a pure stream with bounded memory isn't generally possible for the exact answer. I'd chunk it or, if heights are bounded, exploit that. I'd flag the worst-case space honestly rather than claim O(1)."

- **"Can heights be negative?"**
  > 💬 "Not in this problem — a histogram bar can't have negative height. If the problem redefined them as values that can dip below zero, 'area' wouldn't be well-defined geometrically; I'd ask the interviewer to restate what rectangle means there before coding."

---

## If You Get Stuck

- **Verbalize, don't freeze:** "Let me reframe — for each bar, how far can it stretch before hitting a shorter one?" That sentence alone often unlocks the pattern.
- **Reduce the problem:** start with the O(n²) brute force out loud — "for each bar, expand left and right." Get that correct first; a working O(n²) beats a broken O(n).
- **Pattern-match aloud:** "I need the nearest SHORTER bar on each side — that's nearest-smaller-element, which is a monotonic stack in O(n)." Say the words; they lead to the structure.
- **If the width formula tangles you:** draw two bars and the popped one between them, label left and right as exclusive walls — the `right - left - 1` falls out of the picture, not memory.
